statement: Iceland's EU accession negotiations (2010–2013) were managed by a chief negotiator (Stefán Haukur Jóhannesson, later ambassador and permanent secretary at MFA) overseeing 35 negotiating teams, one per acquis chapter. Each team included civil servants, academics, and sector specialists. The agriculture chapter (Chapter 11) team included specialists from the Ministry of Industries and Innovation, the Agricultural Advisory Centre (Ráðgjafamiðstöð landbúnaðarins), and independent economists. The food safety, veterinary, and phytosanitary chapter (Chapter 12) had a separate team. Several individuals served on multiple teams — it was not uncommon for senior officials or subject-matter experts to sit on two or three teams simultaneously, particularly where chapters overlapped (e.g., agriculture and food safety). The negotiating position on agriculture was developed with significant input from Bændasamtök Íslands (Farmers' Association of Iceland) and the Ministry of Industries.
caveats: The exact composition of each negotiating team varied over time as members rotated. Some team lists were published on the MFA website during negotiations but have since been archived. The 35 teams corresponded to 33 acquis chapters plus two horizontal teams.

related_entries.statement: Iceland applied for EU membership on 16 July 2009, during the aftermath of the financial crisis, under a Social Democrat-led government. Accession negotiations opened in July 2010 and progressed through 33 acquis chapters, of which 27 were opened and 11 provisionally closed by 2013. The most contentious chapters — fisheries (Chapter 13), agriculture (Chapter 11), and free movement of capital (Chapter 4) — remained unresolved. Following the 2013 election, the incoming centre-right coalition suspended negotiations. In March 2015, the government formally asked the EU to remove Iceland from the list of candidate countries. The 2024 Althingi vote for a binding referendum effectively reopened the question without restarting formal negotiations.

Chapter 13 (Fisheries) was the most contentious chapter during Iceland's accession negotiations. The EU's Common Fisheries Policy requires member states to share fishing quotas under the principle of equal access to EU waters. Iceland's fisheries sector accounted for approximately 40% of goods exports and 5–6% of GDP at the time, and the Icelandic ITQ (Individual Transferable Quota) system was considered incompatible with CFP principles without significant adaptation.

When new member states join the EU, they do not receive full Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) direct payments immediately. Under the phasing-in mechanism established for the 2004 enlargement and maintained since, new members start at 25% of the EU-15 level in year one, increasing by 5 percentage points per year (30%, 35%, 40%...) until reaching 100% after approximately 10 years. This was the arrangement for the EU-10 (2004), EU-2 (2007, Bulgaria and Romania), and Croatia (2013). For Croatia specifically, direct payments started at 25% in 2013 and were scheduled to reach full convergence by 2022. The 2023–2027 CAP reform introduced 'external convergence', aiming to bring all member states' average per-hectare payments to at least 90% of the EU average by 2027, which accelerated convergence for the newer members.
